draft.introduction: The Baal Cycle from Ugarit (c. 1400–1200 BCE) narrates the storm god Baal’s battles against Yam (Sea) and Mot (Death), his death and resurrection, and the establishment of his kingship. It is the primary mythological background for understanding biblical imagery of divine combat.

translation.text: The craftsman god Kothar-wa-Khasis fashioned two clubs and declared their names: ‘Your name is Driver—drive Yam from his throne, River from the seat of his dominion!’ The club swooped from the hand of Baal like a raptor. It struck Prince Yam on the skull. Yam collapsed and fell to the earth. Baal scattered and drank Yam.

translator_notes.note: The combat between Baal and Yam (Sea/Chaos) provides the mythological background for biblical passages like Psalm 74:13–14, Isaiah 51:9–10, and Revelation 21:1. The Israelite authors did not simply borrow Canaanite myths but transformed them: YHWH replaces Baal, and the combat becomes creation, not mere kingship.
